title: The Unequal Impact of Firms on the Gender Wage Gap
abstract: Firm-specific wage premiums - the extra pay that similarly skilled workers receive in some firms compared with others - contribute greatly to the gender wage gap in US and European labor markets. Women capture a smaller share of firm surpluses than equally skilled men, and are concentrated in part-time-intensive firms, associated with smaller wage premiums.
summary_en: Men earn more than women partly because employers differ: some firms pay higher wage premiums than others, and women sort into the less-generous firms. This paper uses harmonised matched employer-employee data from the US and several European countries — including Hungary — to show that women capture a smaller share of firm surpluses than equally skilled men, and are disproportionately concentrated in part-time-intensive firms that pay smaller wage premiums. The cross-country comparison quantifies how much of the gender wage gap comes from which firms women work at, not from pay differences within firms.
data_used: Harmonised matched employer-employee administrative data for the US and multiple European countries including Hungary, Germany, Denmark, France, Norway, Italy, Netherlands, Poland, Sweden; spans 2000s-2010s; millions of worker-firm observations.
policy_relevance: Direct Hungarian-policy relevance. For Ministry of Finance and Ministry of Interior (gender-equality portfolios): a significant portion of the Hungarian gender wage gap is about sorting — women are concentrated in lower-premium firms, not just paid less within firms. Policies that improve women's access to high-premium firms (e.g., matching services, childcare enabling career-track jobs) may close the gap faster than within-firm pay-equity regulation alone. Part-time-intensive firms pay smaller premiums, so policies expanding high-quality part-time work in large employers matter.
